content.text: I just dropped two JSON files in perf-reports. Those are lighthouse reports for desktop and mobile from a deployed version of this branch. Can you take a look at them and give some insight into changes I could make. They're similar to what we have set up for our `scripts/perf.sh`, but deployed instead of the local version so they're more accurate (aside from the things that Netlify previews do, which you'll notice)

input.command:
cat perf-reports/deploy-preview-112--tylergaw.netlify.app-20260716T162822.json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
config = data.get('configSettings', {})
emulated = config.get('formFactor', 'unknown')
print(f'Form factor: {emulated}')
print()

cats = data.get('categories', {})
for name, cat in cats.items():
    score = cat.get('score', 0)
    print(f'{cat[\"title\"]}: {int(score*100)}')

print()
print('--- Performance Metrics ---')
audits = data.get('audits', {})
metrics = ['first-contentful-paint', 'largest-contentful-paint', 'total-blocking-time', 'cumulative-layout-shift', 'speed-index', 'interactive']
for m in metrics:
    a = audits.get(m, {})
    print(f'{a.get(\"title\", m)}: {a.get(\"displayValue\", \"N/A\")} (score: {a.get(\"score\", \"N/A\")})')
"

content.text: Good scores overall. Let me dig into the specific audit findings to see what's actionable.

input.command:
cat perf-reports/deploy-preview-112--tylergaw.netlify.app-20260716T163018.json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
audits = data.get('audits', {})

# Show failed/warning audits grouped by category
cats = data.get('categories', {})
for cat_name, cat in cats.items():
    refs = cat.get('auditRefs', [])
    issues = []
    for ref in refs:
        audit = audits.get(ref['id'], {})
        score = audit.get('score')
        if score is not None and score < 1:
            title = audit.get('title', ref['id'])
            display = audit.get('displayValue', '')
            desc = audit.get('description', '')[:120]
            issues.append((score, title, display, ref['id']))
    if issues:
        print(f'=== {cat[\"title\"]} (issues) ===')
        for score, title, display, aid in sorted(issues, key=lambda x: x[0]):
            print(f'  [{score}] {title} {display}')
            # Show details for interesting audits
            details = audits[aid].get('details', {})
            items = details.get('items', [])
            if items and len(items) <= 5:
                for item in items[:5]:
                    if isinstance(item, dict):
                        # try to show url or node info
                        url = item.get('url', item.get('source', {}).get('url', '') if isinstance(item.get('source'), dict) else '')
                        node = item.get('node', {})
                        snippet = node.get('snippet', '') if isinstance(node, dict) else ''
                        label = item.get('label', item.get('description', ''))
                        wastedMs = item.get('wastedMs', '')
                        wastedBytes = item.get('wastedBytes', '')
                        parts = [p for p in [url, snippet, label, f'wasted: {wastedMs}ms' if wastedMs else '', f'wasted: {wastedBytes}B' if wastedBytes else ''] if p]
                        if parts:
                            print(f'        {\" | \".join(parts[:3])}')
        print()
"

cat perf-reports/deploy-preview-112--tylergaw.netlify.app-20260716T162822.json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
audits = data.get('audits', {})

# SEO details
cats = data.get('categories', {})
cat = cats.get('seo', {})
refs = cat.get('auditRefs', [])
print('=== SEO Audit Details (Desktop) ===')
for ref in refs:
    audit = audits.get(ref['id'], {})
    score = audit.get('score')
    if score is not None and score < 1:
        title = audit.get('title', ref['id'])
        desc = audit.get('description', '')[:200]
        print(f'  [{score}] {title}')
        print(f'    {desc}')
        details = audit[\"details\"] if \"details\" in audit else {}
        items = details.get('items', [])
        for item in items[:5]:
            if isinstance(item, dict):
                print(f'    -> {json.dumps(item)[:200]}')
        print()
"

cat perf-reports/deploy-preview-112--tylergaw.netlify.app-20260716T163018.json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
audits = data.get('audits', {})

# Font display details
print('=== Font Display ===')
fd = audits.get('font-display', {})
items = fd.get('details', {}).get('items', [])
for item in items:
    print(f'  {item.get(\"url\", \"\")} | wastedMs: {item.get(\"wastedMs\", \"\")}')

print()
print('=== Render-Blocking Resources ===')
rb = audits.get('render-blocking-resources', {})
items = rb.get('details', {}).get('items', [])
for item in items:
    print(f'  {item.get(\"url\", \"\")} | wastedMs: {item.get(\"wastedMs\", \"\")}')

print()
print('=== Image Delivery ===')
img = audits.get('uses-optimized-images', {})
items = img.get('details', {}).get('items', [])
if not items:
    # try modern-image-formats
    img = audits.get('modern-image-formats', {})
    items = img.get('details', {}).get('items', [])
if not items:
    img = audits.get('image-delivery', {})
    items = img.get('details', {}).get('items', [])
for item in items[:10]:
    url = item.get('url', item.get('node', {}).get('path', ''))
    wb = item.get('wastedBytes', '')
    print(f'  {url[:120]} | wastedBytes: {wb}')

print()
print('=== LCP Discovery ===')
lcp = audits.get('lcp-lazy-loaded', {})
print(f'  Title: {lcp.get(\"title\", \"\")}')
print(f'  Score: {lcp.get(\"score\", \"\")}')
lcp2 = audits.get('prioritize-lcp-image', {})
print(f'  Title: {lcp2.get(\"title\", \"\")}')
print(f'  Score: {lcp2.get(\"score\", \"\")}')
items = lcp2.get('details', {}).get('items', [])
for item in items[:5]:
    print(f'  {json.dumps(item)[:300]}')

print()
print('=== Accessibility - Contrast ===')
contrast = audits.get('color-contrast', {})
items = contrast.get('details', {}).get('items', [])
for item in items[:10]:
    node = item.get('node', {})
    print(f'  {node.get(\"snippet\", \"\")}')
    print(f'    selector: {node.get(\"selector\", \"\")}')
    print(f'    explanation: {node.get(\"explanation\", \"\")}')
"

cat perf-reports/deploy-preview-112--tylergaw.netlify.app-20260716T163018.json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
audits = data.get('audits', {})

# Get all audits with score 0 or 0.5 that have details
for aid, audit in sorted(audits.items()):
    score = audit.get('score')
    if score is not None and score <= 0.5:
        details = audit.get('details', {})
        items = details.get('items', [])
        if items and not aid.startswith('bf-') and 'chrome-extension' not in json.dumps(items):
            print(f'=== {aid} (score: {score}) ===')
            print(f'  Title: {audit.get(\"title\", \"\")}')
            for item in items[:5]:
                print(f'  {json.dumps(item)[:250]}')
            print()
" 2>&1 | head -80

content.text: Here's the breakdown of what's actionable vs. what you can ignore: ## Ignore These - **SEO: "Page is blocked from indexing"** — That's the Netlify deploy preview sending `x-robots-tag: noindex`. Not a real issue; your production site won't have this. - **"Minify JavaScript" / "Reduce unused JavaScript"** — Both items are from a `chrome-extension://` (looks like a password manager). Not your code. - **"Network dependency tree"** — This is informational/insight-level, not a real failure. ## Worth Addressing ### 1. Accessibility: Color Contrast (score: 0) The `#ff0000` on `#fcfcfc` (and vice versa) pairing has a contrast ratio of 3.89:1 — needs 4.5:1. This affects: - All nav links in the footer (`nav.g-main__item > ul > li > a`) - The "about" card and text in `home-contents-tertiary` Fixing this means darkening the red slightly. `#d40000` gets you to ~4.6:1, or `#cc0000` gives ~4.9:1. Small visual change for a meaningful a11y win. ### 2. Font Display (score: 0, ~70ms wasted) `Dragonsteel-Rough.woff2` doesn't have `font-display: swap` (or `optional`/`fallback`). Adding `font-display: swap` to its `@font-face` declaration prevents invisible text during load. ### 3. LCP Image Missing `fetchpriority="high"` (score: 0) The LCP element is `div.inner > div.graphics > div.pane > img` (the hero lady image). Adding `fetchpriority="high"` to that `` tells the browser to prioritize it, which should help mobile LCP (currently 2.9s). ### 4. Render-Blocking CSS (score: 0.5) Several page-specific CSS files are loaded on the homepage but not needed there: `resume.css`, `purpose.css`, `art.css`, `streetcred.css`. The biggest offender is `home.css` at ~150ms wasted. Options: - Load page-specific CSS only on their respective pages (if not already conditional) - Inline critical CSS and async-load the rest ### 5. Image Delivery The home work images (`.home-contents-card` images) are being served larger than displayed. For example, images with `width="1111"` are rendered at 380px wide on mobile. Responsive `srcset` with appropriately sized variants would save significant bytes. The hero images are already `.webp` which is good. --- The contrast fix and `fetchpriority="high"` are quick wins. Want me to look at any of these in the code to start implementing?
I'm not going to change the color constrast, so we'll ignore that one, what's the other(s)

The remaining actionable ones:

1. **Font display** — add `font-display: swap` to `Dragonsteel-Rough`
2. **LCP `fetchpriority="high"`** — add to the hero image
3. **Render-blocking CSS** — page-specific CSS loading on pages that don't need it
4. **Image delivery** — oversized images served to small viewports

Let me look at the code for these.

content.text: OK, here's the situation for each: ### 1. Font display — easy fix Add `font-display: swap` to the `@font-face` at `src/css/tylergaw.css:391-395`. ### 2. LCP `fetchpriority="high"` — tricky The hero image is rendered 15 times (3 groups × 5 via `webc:for`). Lighthouse flagged the one that ends up as the LCP element. Adding `fetchpriority="high"` to one of the first-visible `` tags would help, but since they're generated in a loop it would apply to all of them. That's probably fine — they're all the same image so the browser only fetches it once anyway. ### 3. Render-blocking CSS — structural limitation All page CSS is bundled into one `tylergaw.css` via `@import`. Every page loads every page-specific stylesheet. Splitting this up (per-page CSS loading) would be a bigger architectural change. The files are tiny though (600-3000 bytes each), so the real-world impact is minimal. ### 4. Image delivery — would need sized variants The home work card images are served at 1111px wide but rendered at ~380px on mobile. You'd need to generate smaller image variants and add `srcset`. This is a bigger effort. **My recommendation**: items 1 and 2 are quick, low-risk changes. Items 3 and 4 are more involved with diminishing returns given your scores are already 100 desktop / 94 mobile. Want me to make the font-display and fetchpriority changes?
Cool, yeah #3 is on the list to fix as I'm working and #4 I can do later too. Can you give me a bit more background on the why on numbers 1 and 2

### Font display (`font-display: swap`)

Without a `font-display` value, browsers default to `auto`, which in practice usually means `block` — the browser hides text entirely while the font downloads. So any text using Dragonsteel Rough is invisible until the woff2 finishes loading. On a slow connection that could be a noticeable flash of invisible text.

`font-display: swap` tells the browser to immediately show text using a fallback font, then swap in Dragonsteel once it's ready. The user sees content right away instead of a blank spot. The 70ms Lighthouse flagged is the time text was hidden waiting for the font on that test run. Small on a fast connection, but it compounds on slower ones.

### `fetchpriority="high"` on the LCP image

The browser has to decide what to download first when a page loads — HTML, CSS, fonts, images, scripts are all competing. Images default to a lower priority than CSS and scripts. The LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) element is by definition the biggest visible thing when the page loads, so it's the one that matters most for perceived speed.

`fetchpriority="high"` tells the browser "this image is more important than other images, bump it up in the download queue." Without it, the browser discovers the image in the HTML but may delay fetching it while it finishes lower-priority work. On your mobile report, LCP is 2.9s — part of that is the browser not prioritizing that image early enough.

Lighthouse specifically flagged that the LCP image was discoverable in the initial HTML (good) and eagerly loaded (good), but was missing the priority hint. It's a one-attribute fix for what is often the single biggest factor in perceived load time on mobile.
Question on fetchpriority. For that image, I'm putting it in the DOM like 15 times to achieve a certain effect. Is the browser smart enough to know it's the same image?

Yes. The browser caches by URL, so all 15 `` tags pointing to `/images/home/home-hero-lady.webp` result in a single network request. The first one triggers the fetch, and the rest get the cached response. Adding `fetchpriority="high"` to all 15 (since they're in a loop) is fine too — it's the URL that gets prioritized, not the element. Multiple hints for the same URL don't cause multiple fetches or any conflict.
